# MAIN ARTICLE

ORIGINAL PAPER


# Determination of Cr(VI) based on the peroxidase mimetic catalytic activity of citrate-capped gold nanoparticles


Yingying Qi1 & Jinxin Ma1 & Fu-Rong Xiu1 & Xiang Gao1

Received: 21April2021 /Accepted: 12July2021

# The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer-Verlag GmbH Austria, part of Springer Nature2021


# Abstract


Highly negatively charged gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) are shown to have strong simulated oxidase activity and effectively boosted the oxidation of enzyme substrate3,3 ′ ,5,5 ′ -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) by hexavalent chromium ion Cr(VI), resulting in the formation of oxidation product with blue color. Based on this, a facile colorimetric assay was developed to detect Cr(VI) at a range0.008~0.156mg/L with r = 0.996. The detection limit was estimated to be0.52 μ g/L. In addition, the colorimetric assay showed high selectivity against28other interfering ions. It was performed at room temperature and required about half an hour including the preparation of AuNPs. The assay was successfully applied to the determination of Cr(VI) in spiked water samples, and recoveries in the range95.00 -105.40% were obtained. This work paves a way for design of high performance sensor based on highly active nanozymes and also provides an extremely practical analytical tool for the monitoring of Cr(VI) in the environment.

Keywords Colorimetric sensor . Hexavalent chromium . AuNPs . Environmental water analysis . High affinity mechanism


# Introduction


The maximum allowable limit for Cr(VI) in drinking water is set to be50 μ g/L by the World Health Organization (WHO) [1]. The monitoring of low-concentration Cr(VI) in environment is very important for environmental supervision and prevention of human health. So far, the reported analytical methods for detection of Cr(VI) in aqueous environment mainly i n c l u d e d t r a d i t i o n a l methods s u c h as microextraction-electrothermal atomic absorption spectrometry [2], flame atomic absorption spectrometry [3], mass spectrometry [4] and ion chromatography [5, 6], electrochemical technique [7, 8], surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy [9], fluorescence spectrometry [10 -12], and colorimetric method [13 -21].

Visual or colorimetric assay has been always active because of outstanding simplicity [22, 23]. In recent years, a variety of nanomaterials were found to display enzyme mimicking activity and were used to the colorimetric detection of

* Yingying Qi qiyingying@xust.edu.cn

1College of Geology and Environment, Xi ' an University of Science and Technology, Xi ' an710054, China

many target substances like small molecules glucose and galactose [24 -29]. At the same time, colorimetric analysis based on the simulated oxidase has also been developed to detect Cr (VI). The colorimetric analysis for Cr(VI) detection mostly adopted the strategy of catalytic oxidation of substrate3,3 ′ ,5,5 ′ -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) by nanozyme. In2018, gold amalgam nanocomposites were synthesized for colorimetric quantification of Cr(VI) by using their oxidoreductase-like activity [17]. Nghia et al. reported a colorimetric detection of chromate based on graphene oxide nanoparticles as peroxidase mimetic and8-hydroxyquinoline as an inhibitor [30]. Last year, colorimetric determination of Cr(VI) was also developed by the oxidoreductase-like activity of Fe3O4magnetic nanoparticles [19]. The analysis cannot avoid the defects of magnetically separated heterogeneous systems. Recently, cerium oxide nanorods-based metal-organic frameworks (CeO2NRs-MOF) were prepared, and the catalyzed oxidation of CeO2NRs-MOF for TMB was used to the sensing of Cr(VI) [31]. It was also reported that SA-Fe/NG as mimetic peroxidase was prepared for the colorimetric sensing of Cr(VI) using8-hydroxyquinoline (8-HQ) as inhibitor [32]. The nanomaterial-based colorimetric methods for Cr(VI) detection are summarized in Table S1. These analyses demonstrated high sensitivity. However, the synthesis of t h e used nanomaterials was cumbersome and

time-consuming. Each preparation needed several hours, and some even took dozens of hours, which made the analysis lumpish. It is the current breakthrough of the colorimetric detection of chromium to explore the nanozyme probe which is simple to prepare and has high catalytic activity.

Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) have excellent catalytic properties due to their ability to promote the transfer of materials and electrons through the interface of nanoparticles [33, 34]. Previous studies inferred that electrostatic interaction was an important factor affecting the catalytic activity of AuNPs for chemical reactions [33, 35]. Then, what relationship exists between the properties of AuNPs and the catalytic oxidation of enzyme substrate TMB by Cr(VI) ion? It is well known that Cr(VI) has strong oxidation property due to the redox potential of Cr(VI)/Cr(III) (1.36V) [36]. The oxidation reaction of TMBusually occurs under acidic conditions, and TMB mainly exists in the form of cation [37, 38]. Considering that the strong electrostatic attraction favors the interaction between reactants, AuNPs with highly negative charge were prepared in this work. It was reported that AuNPs exhibited peroxidase mimicking activity [24]. In this study, it was found that the highly electronegative AuNPs could significantly catalyze the oxidation of TMB by Cr(VI) to produce blue TMB oxide. Based on this, a rapid and simple visual or colorimetric analysis for Cr(VI) was developed. Cr(VI) as low as93.6 μ g/L was observed to cause color change in the solution by the naked eye. Using the absorbance, the detection limit for Cr (VI) ion was0.52 μ g/L. The US Environmental Protection Agency limits the maximum contaminant level of chromium in environmental water to be100ppb [39]. The sensitivity of the proposed assay is sufficient to meet this requirement. The sensitivity is mainly attributed to the significant enhancement of the catalytic activity of nanozymes by strong electrostatic attraction between the highly electronegative AuNPs and TMB cations. Moreover, the preparation of AuNPs as nanozymes is very simple, and the response to color reaction is fast. For the whole analysis including the preparation of nanozymes, a single assay can be completed in half an hour. From Table S1, although this method is simple, its sensitivity is not better than other colorimetric methods [14, 21, 32]. The analysis is highly selective for Cr(VI) detection. The detection results of real water samples confirmed the reliability of the analysis in practical application. Indeed, the developed analysis is an extremely useful analytical tool for daily online monitoring of Cr(VI) in the environment.


# Experimental section



# Chemicals and instruments


K2Cr2O7was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and dissolved to

prepare the Cr(VI) ion stock solution. The working Cr(VI) ion solution was obtained by serial dilution of the stock solution. Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4·4H2O), 3, 3, 5, 5-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), sodium citrate, and sodium borohydride were also purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). Acetic acid (HAc), sodium acetate (NaAc), and nitrates of other metal ions (Ba2+ , Gd3+ , Ho3+ , K + , Ag + , Lu3+ , Na + , Nd3+ , Mn2+ , Co2+ , Ni2+ , Cd2+ , Zn2+ , Pb2+ , Hg2+ , Cu2+ , Fe2+ , Mg2+ , Al3+ , Cr3+ , and Fe3+ ) were purchased from Xi ' an Chemical Reagents Company. Other metal ion solutions were obtained by dissolving the corresponding nitrates. All reagents used in this work were analytical grade and used without further purification. Ultrapure water (18M Ω cm -1 ) was used in all experiments.

UV-Visible absorption spectra of all the test solutions were scanned by a Hitachi U-3900H UV-Visible Spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) at room temperature. All the photographs were obtained by an Olympus C-370digital camera. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of different AuNPs were measured with a JEM-2100TEM (Japan Electronics Co., Ltd.) at ambient conditions by deposition of AuNPs solution on copper grids. Zeta potentials of different AuNPs were measured by using a Malvern Zetasizer2000. Three rounds of operations were carried out to obtain the average data. A model PB-10digital ion analyzer (Sartorius Scientific instruments Co., Ltd., Beijing, China) was used to adjust the pH value of the buffer solution. Ultrapure water (18M Ω cm -1 ) used in all the experiments was obtained by a Millipore Milli-Q water purification system (Billerica, MA). Flame atomic absorption spectrometry (FAAS) was used to detect Cr(VI) by an Analyst-200atomic absorption spectrometer (PerkinElmer, Waltham, USA). The wavelength was set to357.9nm, and air/ acetylene flame was used with air flow of10.0L/min and acetylene flow of4.0L/min.


# Synthesis of negatively charged AuNPs and positively charged AuNPs


In the proposed sensing, negatively charged AuNPs were used as oxidoreductase mimic to catalyze the oxidation of substrate TMB by Cr(VI) ions. According to previous studies [22, 34], citrate-coated AuNPs with highly negative charge were prepared by sodium citrate reduction method of one-step addition. In order to clarify the correlation between the high electronegativity of AuNPs and the activity of oxidoreductase mimics, positively charged AuNPs modified by amino group were also prepared as a comparison. The positively charged AuNPs were prepared according to our previous work [33, 35].


# Cr(VI) sensing


The colorimetric sensing for Cr(VI) was carried out at room temperature by mixing reagents in one step and incubating for3min. In detail, 30 μ L of TMB (4mM), 30 μ L of negatively charged AuNPs (17nM), and30 μ L of Cr(VI) ion solutions with different concentrations (0.008~0.156mg/L) were mixed in910 μ Lof0.2M acetate buffer (pH = 3.0) and incubated for3min at room temperature to conduct the oxidation reaction of substrate TMB. Then, 250 μ L of the color reacted solutions was decanted into a slit quartz cuvette of350 μ L volume for scanning UV-Vis absorption spectrum at room temperature. Using the absorbance value at the wavelength of652nm, the linear standard curve of the detection of Cr(VI) was obtained, and the Cr(VI) concentration was quantified. The concentrations of Cr(VI) used in the calibration curve are the final concentration of Cr(VI) in the reaction solution. The main reason for using30 μ L is that it can make the total volume an integer value of1mL (30 μ L Cr(VI) + 30 μ L TMB+30 μ L AuNPs +910 μ L buffer solution), which is convenient for the calculation of final Cr(VI) concentration. It agreed with previous literatures [17, 18].

For the genuine water sample, it needed to be filtered by the filter membrane before detection. The absorbance was measured by the above-mentioned method. And then, the amount of Cr(VI) was calculated using the linear standard curve.


# Results and discussion



# Characterization of negatively charged AuNPs and positively charged AuNPs


The two AuNPs were characterized respectively. The results are shown in Fig. 1A. The results of UV-Vis absorption spectra showed that the two AuNPs had the corresponding maximum absorption at the wavelength of about520 -530nm, which was the characteristic absorption peak of AuNPs solution [34, 40]. The characteristic absorption wavelength of AuNPs changed slightly due to different particle sizes. The inserted TEM images showed that the two AuNPs presented similar spherical nanoparticles in appearance. The size distribution of AuNPs was calculated by counting200particles. The average particle size of negatively charged AuNPs was about15 ± 1.5nm, which was smaller than that of positively charged AuNPs (20 ± 2.1nm). It is known that zeta potential is an important parameter indicating the surface charge properties of particles. The measurement results of zeta potential showed that the zeta potential of AuNPs wrapped by citrate was -42.3 ± 3.2mV, while the zeta potential of amino modified AuNPs was +38.6 ± 3.8mV, which agreed with the results of previous studies [33, 35]. The two AuNPs at pH = 3 (the pH condition of the proposed analysis) were also characterized respectively. The results are shown in Fig. 1B. UV-Vis absorption spectra and TEM results showed that there was no obvious difference between the prepared AuNPs and the AuNPs at pH = 3. The zeta potentials of negatively charged AuNPs and positively charged AuNPs at pH = 3were measured to be -38.1 ± 2.9mV and + 41.3 ± 3.3mV, respectively, which were slightly higher than those of the prepared ones ( -42.3 ± 3.2mV and + 38.6 ± 3.8mV). It agreed with the previous research [24]. Also, the two AuNPs at pH = 3still maintained their original charged properties. The different charged properties on the surface of the AuNPs certainly determined the completely different electrostatic interactions (attraction or repulsion) between the AuNPs and the reacted ions in the catalytic oxidation of substrate TMB by Cr(VI) ions, which extremely likely led to completely different activities of simulated enzyme.


# Negatively charged AuNPs ' catalyzed oxidation for reaction of Cr(VI) and TMB


Many studies have been reported that gold nanomaterials exhibit strong catalytic ability in various chemical reactions [24, 33, 34, 41]. Jv et al. found that positively charged AuNPs possessed peroxidase-like activity and could catalyze the oxidation of substrate TMB by H2O2 [24]. Herefrom, it is speculated that AuNPs may have oxidase-like activity and can catalyze the oxidation reaction of substrate TMB and Cr(VI) ions to produce blue oxidation products. The possible mechanism for catalytic oxidation of TMB and Cr(VI) by AuNPs as oxidase mimics is shown in Scheme1. A series of experiments were conducted to verify this speculation. The results found that the reaction of TMB and Cr(VI) ions in the absence of AuNPs was very slow and weak. In a short time (several minutes), the solution of TMB and Cr(VI) ions almost had no color change, and the corresponding UV-Vis absorption spectra showed that the absorption intensity at the wavelength of about650nm was very weak (Fig. 2b). When negatively charged AuNPs were added to the system, the system solution of TMB+Cr(VI)+negatively charged AuNPs appeared intense blue in minutes, and the UV-Vis absorption spectrum also had very strong absorption at the wavelength of650nm (Fig. 2d). Obviously, the negatively charged AuNPs added greatly promoted the generation of TMB oxide, resulting in a clear blue solution. Then, in the TMB oxidation reaction, does negatively charged AuNPs really participate in gain and loss of electron or promote the electron transfer? This determines whether the colorimetric analysis can be used for sensing Cr(VI) ions. To exclude the possibility of negatively charged AuNPs participating in gain and loss of electron in TMB oxidation reaction, the reaction system of TMB+negatively charged AuNPs in the absence of Cr(VI) was investigated. The results (Fig. 2a) showed that the reaction solution of TMB+negatively charged AuNPsdid not have any color change. It indicated that no blue

Fig. 1 (A) UV-Visible absorption spectra of the prepared negatively charged AuNPs (a) and the prepared positively charged AuNPs (b). (B) UV-Visible absorption spectra of negatively charged AuNPs (a) and

TMBoxidewasgenerated, and no oxidation reaction occurred between negatively charged AuNPs and TMB, and negatively charged AuNPs could not get electrons from TMB to participate in the oxidation reaction. The experimental results fully verified that negatively charged AuNPs acted as oxidase

positively charged AuNPs (b) at pH = 3. Inset: TEM images and the corresponding zeta potential results

mimics and had strong catalytic activity in the oxidation reaction of TMB by Cr(VI) ions.

The following mechanisms lied behind this phenomenon. The redox potential of Cr6+ /Cr3+ is1.36V and that of oxTMB/ TMB is1.13V [42]. The oxidation reaction between Cr(VI)

Fig. 2UV-Visible absorption spectra for catalytic oxidation solution of substrate TMB under different cases: (a) ( -) AuNPs, (b) Cr6+ ions, (c) (+) AuNPs+Cr6+ ions, and (d) ( -) AuNPs+Cr6+ ions. The inset shows the corresponding images. Experimental conditions for a: ( -) AuNPs concentration, 17nM, 30 μ L. Conditions for b: Cr6+ ions concentration, 0.520mg/L, 30 μ L. Conditions for c: Cr6+ ions concentration, 0.520mg/L, 30 μ L and (+) AuNPs concentration, 16nM, 30 μ L. Conditions for d: Cr6+ ions concentration, 0.520mg/L, 30 μ L and ( -) AuNPs concentration, 17nM, 30 μ L. In all these cases, 30 μ L of4mM TMBsolution was used, and the mixed solutions were added in910 μ Lof0.2M acetate buffer (pH = 3.0) and incubated for3min at room temperature

ions and TMB can take place. It is known that AuNPs can significantly facilitate electron transfer in many chemical reactions [33, 34, 43]. On the one hand, by the same principle, the electron transfer between Cr(VI) ions and TMB is easier and more convenient by using nanoparticle interface. The nanoparticle-mediated electron transfer (between oxidant and reductant) endows negatively charged AuNPs to exhibit oxidation-mimicking enzyme activity. On the other hand, the oxidation reaction of Cr(VI) ions with TMB occurred under acidic conditions. Under the condition, TMB mainly existed in the form of cations, and chromium existed in the form of HCrO4 -. Since the amount of TMB (30 μ L of4mM) in the reaction system was much greater than that of chromium (30 μ L of0.15~3 μ M), the main body in the reaction solution was TMB cation. The strong electrostatic attraction between the AuNPs with high negative charge and the TMB cations promoted the proximity of the substrate TMB and gold nanozyme, thus effectively improving the electron transfer efficiency. As a consequence, negatively charged AuNPs displayed strong catalytic activity. As a contrast, positively charged AuNPs were prepared, and their catalytic activity for TMB oxidation by Cr(VI) ions was investigated. As expected, few blue oxidation products were found in the reaction solution of TMB+Cr(VI)+positively charged AuNPs (Fig. 2c), and the corresponding UV-Vis absorption spectrum also had very weak absorption at650nm wavelength. The positively charged AuNPs showed very weak catalytic activity for the oxidation of TMB with Cr(VI) ions. The results further confirmed that the electrostatic interaction had important effect on the catalytic activity of AuNPs as nanozymes. In conclusion, the strong oxidase mimicking activity displayed by negatively charged AuNPs is mainly due to that the strong electrostatic attraction between highly negatively charged AuNPs and TMB cations effectively improves the electron transfer efficiency mediated by AuNPs.


# High selectivity of Cr(VI) for TMB oxidation catalyzed by negatively charged AuNPs


The selectivity of oxidase mimics ' activity for Cr(VI) ions is the premise of this colorimetric analysis for highly selective sensing Cr(VI). The selectivity of the colorimetric analysis for Cr(VI) ion was investigated through two modes. One was to investigate the chromogenic response of Cr(VI) ion and other ions to the system solution. The other was to survey the effect of different ions on chromogenic solution after mixing with Cr (VI) ion. The result is shown in Fig. 3. The concentration of Cr (VI) ion was0.13mg/L (the equivalent of2.5 μ M), and the concentration (25 μ M) of other ions (Ba2+ , Gd3+ , Ho3+ , K + , Ag + , Lu3+ , Na + , Nd3+ , Mn2+ , Co2+ , Ni2+ , Cd2+ , Zn2+ , Pb2+ , Hg2+ , Cu2+ , Fe2+ , Mg2+ , Al3+ , Cr3+ , Fe3+ , F -, Cl -, Br -, CO3 2 -, NO3 -, SO4 2 -, and OH -) was10-fold of that of Cr(VI). All test conditions were the same. From Fig. 3a, it can be seen that only the system of Cr(VI) ion appears intense blue, while the other solutions appear almost colorless. Correspondingly, only the Cr(VI) ion solution responded to significantly strong absorbance, while the other ions responded to much weaker absorbance (Fig. 3a). In addition, the anti-interference experiment results (Fig. 3b) also found that different ions mixed with Cr(VI) had no obvious effect on the absorbance of the system. The experimental results showed that Cr(VI) ions could selectively boost the oxidase-like activity of negatively charged AuNPs. Besides, the effects of other interfering ions of100times Cr(VI) ion (the concentration of Cr(VI) ion was2.0 μ M (0.104mg/L)) on the system were also investigated. As shown in the Supporting Information, Fig. S1, only the system from Fe3+ ions responded to relatively large absorbance. It showed that Fe3+ ions had some interference to the system, indicating that this analysis was not suitable for the detection of Cr(VI) in environmental systems containing large amounts of Fe3+ ions.

The selectivity of this colorimetric analysis for Cr(VI) ion can be mostly attributed to the following two aspects. (1) The redox potential of Cr(VI)/Cr(III) is1.36V, which is higher than that of other ions (0.77V of Fe(III)/Fe(II), 0.15V of Cu (II)/Cu(I), and0.68V of H2O2). This facilitated the gain and loss of electrons in the reaction process, which made the oxidation reaction easier and the color of the solution more intense. (2) The acceleration effect of Cr(VI) ions for the

Fig. 3a Absorbance of the reaction solutions in the presence of Cr(VI) and other ions and b recovery of Cr6+ for the ( -) AuNPs-catalyzed TMB system with the presence of other ions. Experimental conditions: TMB concentration, 4mM, 30 μ L and ( -) AuNPs concentration, 17nM, 30 μ L. The concentration (25 μ M) of other ions (Ba2+ , Gd3 + , Ho3+ , K + , Ag + , Lu3+ , Na + , Nd3 + , Mn2+ , Co2+ , Ni2+ , Cd2+ , Zn2+ , Pb2+ , Hg2+ , Cu2+ , Fe2+ , Mg2+ , Al3 + , Cr3+ , Fe3+ , F -, Cl -, Br -, CO3 2 -, NO3 -, SO4 2 -, and OH -) was10fold of that of Cr(VI) (2.5 μ M(the equivalent of0.13mg/L)). The mixed solutions were incubated in910 μ L of0.2M acetate buffer (pH = 3.0) for3min at room temperature. All error bars indicate the standard deviation for three measurements

enzyme-catalyzed reaction was investigated by the steady-state kinetics. Taking common hydrogen peroxide as model, the concentrations of enzyme substrate TMB and negatively charged AuNPs as oxidase mimic were kept unchanged, and the concentrations of Cr(VI) ion and hydrogen peroxide were changed respectively to conduct the steady-state kinetics experiment. The variation of absorbance value at652nm per unit t i me was taken as t h e i n i t i a l velocity. The Michaelis-Menten curves are shown in the Supporting Information, Fig. S2. The Michaelis-Menten constant (Km) and maximum initial velocity (Vmax) are obtained from Lineweaver-Burk plots (1/V = (Km/Vmax) [S] + 1/Vmax) of the Michaelis-Menten equation (V=Vmax[S]/(Km + [S]). Michaelis-Menten constant (Km) is an important parameter, which can reflect the affinity between enzyme and substrate. WhentheKmvalueis smaller, the affinity between enzyme and substrate is greater, and the enzyme activity is stronger [44]. It can be seen from the results in Table1that the Km value of Cr (VI) ion (0.454) is less than that of hydrogen peroxide (4.375),


<table><caption><div class="caption">Table 1 Comparison of Michaelis constant (Km) and maximal reaction velocity (Vmax) in different systems</div></caption><tbody><tr><th>Catalyst</th><th>Substance</th><th>Km ( μ M)</th><th>Vmax (nM S - 1 )</th></tr><tr><td>( - ) AuNPs</td><td>Cr 6+</td><td>0.454</td><td>30.69</td></tr><tr><td>( - ) AuNPs</td><td>H 2 O 2</td><td>4.375</td><td>25.00</td></tr></tbody></table>


Table1Comparison of Michaelis constant (Km) and maximal reaction velocity (Vmax) in different systems

indicating stronger affinity between Cr(VI) ion and nanozyme of negatively charged AuNPs and stronger catalytic activity of oxidase mimic in Cr(VI) system. Therefore, Cr(VI) ion could significantly enhance the oxidase-like activity of negatively charged AuNPs. Moreover, the maximum initial velocity (Vmax) of Cr(VI) system (30.69) is also higher than that of hydrogen peroxide system (25.00), which also explains the faster color rendering in Cr(VI) system. In short, the high redox potential and the strong affinity of gold nanozymes with Cr(VI) are the main reasons of high selectivity for Cr(VI) ions.


# Detection performance of the proposed colorimetric sensing for Cr(VI)


It can be inferred from the reaction mechanism that the solution color (absorbance) of analysis system depends on the amount of Cr(VI) under certain enzymatic reaction conditions. In order to make the colorimetric analysis more sensitive, the experimental conditions affecting the response of Cr(VI) were optimized. Negatively charged AuNPs as nanozyme are an important factor affecting signal response. It is known that the activity of enzymes is closely related to the sensing response of the system, and the nanozymes of high activity favor the sensitive response to Cr(VI). The effects of particle size and concentration of AuNPs on catalytic activity were investigated. As shown in Supporting Information, Fig. S3a, negatively charged AuNPs with too large or too small particle size did not show high catalytic activity, which agreed with

the previous studies [34]. The higher the concentration of negatively charged AuNPs was, the more intense the color response of the reaction system was. Considering the reagent consumption and the wine red color of AuNPs itself, citrate-coated negatively charged AuNPs with15nm particle diameter and17nM concentration were selected as oxidase mimic for sensing Cr(VI). In addition, the pH value of the reaction system is another important factor for affecting the enzymatic reaction. The effect of pH value on sensing system was investigated. The result is shown in the Supporting Information, Fig. S3b. At pH = 3the absorbance of the solution was maximum; above and below this value, the absorbance tended to decrease. It may be attributed to two aspects. On the one hand, hexavalent chromium can exist in different forms like H2CrO4, HCrO4 -, CrO4 2 -, and Cr2O7 2 -. The first-order dissociation constant (pK a1 ) of chromic acid is4.1 [45]. In acidic system (pH = 3), hexavalent chromium mainly existed in the form of H2CrO4or HCrO4 -, which had strong oxidizing ability and was suitable for TMB oxidation reaction. So, the color response of the system was the intensest, and the corresponding absorbance was the maximum. On the other hand, TMB oxidation reaction is carried out in two steps. TMB is first oxidized to the blue product of intermediate radical (the single electron oxidation) at pH2 -4acidic conditions. When the pH value is increased to weak acid or neutral conditions, it is further oxidized to quinonediimine or azo compounds [38, 45]. Thus, the acidic system (pH = 3) favors the formation of blue oxidation products. So, acetate buffer (pH = 3) was selected as the optimum medium. Of course, TMB concentration also affects the color response of the solution. It was found that the absorbance of the system gradually increased with the increase of TMB concentration (see the Supporting Information, Fig. S3c). The optimal concentration of TMB was chosen as4mM. Besides, higher or lower temperature will weaken the catalytic activity of AuNPs. Considering the convenience of operation, the whole analysis was carried out at room temperature.

Under the above determined experimental conditions, the correlation between the color response of the system solution and the Cr(VI) concentration was investigated. The results (Fig. 4A) showed that the blue color of the solution became more intense with the increase of Cr(VI) ion concentration. The results of UV-Vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 4B) showed that with the increase of Cr(VI) concentration, the intensity of absorption peak gradually enhanced, and the absorbance at652nm gradually increased (Fig. 4C). In the range of0.008~0.156mg/L (the equivalent of0.15~3 μ M), the Cr (VI) concentration was linearly correlated with the absorbance value (A652). The calibration plot is shown in Fig. 4D. The detection limit for Cr(VI) was estimated to be0.52 μ g/L by the standard deviation of3times for blank solution. The recently reported nanomaterial-based methods for the determination of Cr(VI) were summarized, as shown in the

Supporting Information, Table S1. The proposed assay was comparable to the previously reported nanozyme colorimetric assays [18, 31]. Due to simple preparation and fast response of negatively charged AuNPs, the test speed was much more rapid. Including the preparation of AuNPs, the assay required about half an hour of analysis time, which was much less than that of other analyses (it took hours, even dozens of hours).

In addition, the chromium solutions of different concentrations used in the calibration curve were obtained by diluting the stock solution with buffer solution. In the assay operation, the30 μ L chromium solutions diluted by buffer solution were added to910 μ L buffer solutions for reaction, which corresponded to the different concentrations of chromium solution in the present calibration curve by diluting with buffer solution. And chromium solutions were diluted with buffer solution and real lake water, respectively, and then detected. The results (Fig. 5) found that they were basically the same, which meant that real lake water sample had no interference for the analysis system. Calibration curves were also obtained by diluting chromium solution directly with lake water. It was found to be consistent with the calibration curve obtained by diluting chromium solution with the buffer solution.

Additionally, natural water has abundant Ca2+ and Mg2+ ions, which may affect the stability of AuNPs solution [46, 47]. It is known that the spectral changes shown by the absorbance ratio (A520/A650) of AuNPs solution can indicate the stability of AuNPs [22, 23]. The larger the absorbance ratio (A520/A650) is, the more stable AuNPs are. Conversely, AuNPs are aggregated [22, 23]. The tolerance of AuNPs to Ca2+ and Mg2+ ions was investigated. The results are shown in the Supporting Information, Fig. S4a. When the total concentration of Ca2+ and Mg2+ ions was less than1.3mM, the absorbance ratio (A520/A650) of AuNPs solution was large and kept basically constant, indicating that AuNPs were in a stable dispersion state. While the total concentration of Ca2+ and Mg2+ ions was more than1.3mM, the absorbance ratio (A520/A650) decreased significantly, indicating the occurrence of unstable and aggregated AuNPs. The experimental results demonstrated that Ca2+ and Mg2+ ions (the total concentration ≥ 1.3mM) could affect the stability of AuNPs, which agreed with previous studies [46, 47]. The effect of Ca2+ and Mg2+ ions on the system catalyzed by AuNPs was also explored. The results (see Supporting Information, Fig. S4b) found that when the total concentration of Ca2+ and Mg2+ ions was more than1.3mM, the absorbance of the color solution decreased obviously, indicating that the catalytic activity of AuNPs on enzyme substrate was also weakened after the stability of AuNPs was destroyed. The total concentration of Ca2+ and Mg2+ ions in natural water is about1mM [46, 48]. In this assay, 30 μ L of chromium solution was added to a reaction solution with total volume of1000 μ L (see the ' Cr(VI) sensing ' in the ' Experimental section ' ), which was consistent with previous studies [17 -19]. This is equivalent to about33

Fig. 4A The photographs of the color change of sensing solutions with different concentrations of Cr(VI) from0.008to1.404mg/L. B UV-Vis absorption spectra of the colorimetric sensing system under different concentrations of Cr(VI) from0to1.404mg/L. C The dependence of the absorbance at652nm on the concentration of target Cr(VI) from0to1.404mg/L. D The calibration plot between the absorbance at652nm

times dilution of water sample. Thus, Ca2+ and Mg2+ in diluted natural water did not affect the analysis. Therefore, water samples with low concentration Cr(VI) can be determined with no need of dilution. For water samples with high concentration Cr(VI), it is necessary to dilute it to the linear concentration range of Cr(VI) response before detection.


# Application to real environmental water samples


Lake water and tap water were used as real environmental water samples. The lake water samples were taken from Qujiang

and Cr(VI) concentration from0.008to0.156mg/L. Experimental conditions: TMB concentration, 4mM, 30 μ L and ( -) AuNPs concentration, 17nM, 30 μ L. In all these cases, 30 μ LofCr(VI) solution was added, and the mixed solutions were incubated in910 μ L of0.2M acetate buffer (pH = 3.0) for3min at room temperature. All error bars indicate the standard deviation for three measurements

Lake in Xi ' an and filtered to remove suspended solids before use. The tap water samples were collected from our laboratory and were not treated before use. To investigate the application of this analysis to environmental water samples, the Cr(VI) stock solutions were diluted with NaAc-HAc buffer solution, tap water, and lake water, respectively, and the diluted solutions were determined by this method. As shown in Fig. 5, there was no significant difference in the detection results of Cr(VI) solutions diluted by the three water samples. The experimental results indicated that it was feasible to apply this analysis to detecting Cr(VI) ion in real environment water samples.

Fig. 5Comparison of the colorimetric assay for the detection of Cr(VI) in buffer and real water samples. Experimental conditions: TMB concentration, 4mM, 30 μ L and ( -) AuNPs concentration, 17nM, 30 μ L. In all these cases, 30 μ L of Cr(VI) diluted solution was added, and the mixed solutions were incubated in910 μ L of0.2M acetate buffer (pH = 3.0) for3min at room temperature. All error bars indicate the standard deviation for three measurements

In order to further investigate the analytical application in real environmental water, the spiked recovery experiments were carried out for lake water and tap water. Three different concentrations (52.00 μ g/mL, 104.00 μ g/mL, and156.00 μ g/ mL) in the linear concentration range were taken as the spiked Cr(VI) concentrations. Referring to the literature [18, 19], standard chromium solutions at different concentration levels were spiked to undiluted lake water and tap water samples at the beginning of the whole analytical process and then detected using the developed colorimetric method. The results are shown in Table2. The recoveries ranged from95.00to105.40% for different spiked water samples, and the

Table2Determination of Cr6+ in water samples by the proposed method and FAAS

relative standard deviations (RSD) for six parallel assays were less than or equal to4.2%. In addition, the wastewater was also used as the actual environmental water sample to investigate the practical application of the analysis. Wastewater was taken from Xi ' an third sewage treatment plant in Xi ' an City. After the collected wastewater was filtered through the0.22mm membrane, a certain amount of Cr (VI) standard solution was spiked to obtain the test water sample. The test water samples were determined by this method and atomic absorption spectrometry respectively. As listed in Table2, the recoveries of spiked Cr(VI) ranged from103.46to105.58% with RSD ≤ 4.2%. Experimental results demonstrated that this analysis had good accuracy and reliability for environmental water samples. For the same water sample, the results from this analysis and atomic absorption spectrometry were almost consistent with each other. The experimental results further confirmed the practicability of the analysis for the detection of Cr(VI) ion in the actual environmental water. The assay is handy and inexpensive. It is particularly suitable for monitoring toxic Cr(VI) in environmental water in developing areas.

According to previous studies [17, 18], different experiments could be used to survey the practical application of analysis method. Figure5and Table2are the results of different experiments conducted to survey the practical application of the analysis. Figure5shows the detected results of the proposed assay for Cr(VI) solutions diluted by buffer solution and real water sample. This experiment aimed to investigate the effect of real water sample on Cr(VI) detection by comparing the detection results of Cr(VI) solutions diluted by real water sample and buffer solution. Table2shows the results of the spiked recovery experiments for real water samples, and the practical application of this analysis was investigated through the recovery ratio. The results of the two experiments were consistent with each other, which sufficiently confirmed the feasibility of the analysis in practical application.

# Conclusions


Anovel assay was developed for the colorimetric detection of Cr (VI) by using strong mimic oxidase activity of highly negatively charged AuNPs, which could accelerate the oxidation of substrate TMB by Cr(VI) along with the color change from colorless to blue. The investigation of the reaction mechanism showed that the strong simulated enzyme activity of highly negatively charged AuNPs was derived from the effective promotion of nanoparticle-mediated electron transfer by the strong electrostatic attraction between highly negatively charged AuNPs and TMB cations. This assay presented excellent sensitivity and high selectivity. The whole colorimetric sensing was carried out at room temperature, and the detection of Cr(VI) could be completed in half an hour. This analysis is very rapid and simple and has potential application prospects for real-time and online monitoring Cr(VI) ion in environmental water samples. More importantly, this study provides a new perspective for the construction of sensor based on nanozyme of high activity.
